sentences.text: Good afternoon.
My name is Sala Hudes, H-U-D-E-S.
My name at birth was Sala Kleinberg.
K-L-E-I-N-B-E-R-G.
Yes, I had a name Ajzenberg from my first husband.
The spelling was A-J-Z-E-N-B-E-R-G, which was changed to E-I-S-E-N-B-E-R-G.
No, none.
I was born 10/23/1920.
I am 75.
I was born in Warsaw, W-A-R-S-O-W, Poland.
My family consisted of my mother, Basia, my father, Idel, my three sisters-- I mean, my two sisters and myself, we were three children-- Hana, Regina, and myself, Sala.
Life for Jews, in general for poor people, and Jews were mostly poor, was very, very bad.
But we had a loving family.
We were very poor, but my father always did his best to support his family.
We didn't have much luxury, but we had a lot of love.
We also have lived with us my father's mother.
She was widowed.
And she lived with us all the years since I could remember.
Her name was Malka.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I grew up in a poor neighborhood.
I went to school, which was mostly a [?
Gentile ?] neighborhood.
I finished public school.
And soon after, I went to work.
Well, my-- my school was an elementary school.
And it was very strict, not like today.
We had respect for our teachers, for our elderly, for the parents.
And education was very much, at that time, taken care of.
Primary was education.
I couldn't go higher because, after you finished the public school, you had to pay.
All the other schools were private.
And of course, my parents could not afford it.
Also, after school, I used to tutor a retarded girl.
They-- it was a back of the house at the courtyard where we lived.
And the apartment house was a bakery.
The baker's daughter, she was about five years younger than I was, and she was a little retarded and needed help in school.
So every day after I did my homework, I used to go to the bakery and help his daughter.
I can't recall her name.
Help with the lessons.
For that, I used to get paid $3, like three [NON-ENGLISH] a week.
Which I never collected, because my mother would go every morning and get bread and rolls for the family.
By the end of the week, there was nothing for me to collect because she used up all the money in bread.
And after I finished school, and I went to work, I went to work with a dressmaker.
When I finished school, I was 14.
And I went to work to a dressmaker, learning, worked very hard.
And got paid a little of nothing.

And still after work, when I returned home, before I used to go home, I used to stop at the bakery and help his daughter with the lesson so my mother would continue taking bread everyday, bread, a roll, whatever, a [NON-ENGLISH] for Saturday.
And that's the way I used to help out my parents.
Well, my parents were very simple people.
My father had no education, and so did my mother.
They had no education.
But they were very good parents.
In the wintertime, my father didn't work because my father was a saddle maker.
And in most of winter, he had no work.
And sometimes, he used to go to shovel snow, clean the streets, so he could get some money to support us.
In the time that he didn't work, he used to play with us.
Not only with us, but I remember neighbor's children used to come up.
And he used to play with all of us.
He used to-- we used to cover his eyes, and we used to slap him on the behind.
And he had to guess who did it.
When he guessed, then the next person would sit down in his place.
And if he didn't, he would see sit as long as he guessed who did it.
That was our entertainment.
My mother used to make me a doll.
She used to take old stockings and put in it a lot of old rags and paint eyes and a nose and sew on [INAUDIBLE] made from rags.
And that's what was the dolls.
That's what we played with.
That was the luxury.
But we had a lot of love.
And that's how I grew up.
Well, as I think back, I wasn't exactly a child that I would be today if I think back.
I used to do some mischief.
But of course, you know, my parents took it with love.
I remember also, one time-- we lived across the street from the river Wisla.
I used to go with my friends across the street to the river.
And we used to go in boats that were tied down.
And we-- when a ship used to go by, it would swing.
So we used to sit in the boat swinging.
Once, I told my father that I'm going to the river.
So as we we're having dinner-- and dinner usually consisted of just of a plate of soup and a piece of bread-- my father warned me not to go down.
But this-- and I promised, I wouldn't.
But as soon as we finished dinner, my friends came and called me.
And of course, I went across the river.
And I was afraid to go in the boat, because I promised not to.
So I sat down by the river.
But as I sat down, one of the boys passed by me and gently pushed me in the back.
And I went down to the river, all the way down.
Being that there was a lot of people, I couldn't swim.
There was a lot of people, and somebody jumped in, grabbed me by the hair and pulled me out.
As I was crossing the street dripping with water, it was [INAUDIBLE].
I was shivering.
My mother was sitting on this-- in the yard with neighbors talking.
And she saw me coming on and dripping.
She asked me what happened.
Being that I was afraid to tell the truth, I told her I fell in the gutter.
And then my friend said, no, it's not true.
She was drowning.
So my mother took me upstairs, covered with me with a heavy blanket.
In the meantime, my father was coming home.
And he already found out in the courtyard from the neighbors how lucky he is that I didn't drown.
As soon as he came into the house, he asked my mother what's wrong with me.
My mother said that I have a headache.
She knew that I'm going to get a spanking.
So my father says, well, in one minute, I'm going to cure her headache.
And we always had hanging by the window those whips.
And they were consisted not from one, but there was about 10 or 12 tied together.
So my father took the-- the cover, throw it over my head, and I got such a whipping.
He said, as long as you going to remember, you're going to-- as long as you're going to live, you're going to remember not to lie.
And I'm still remembering.
If that would happen today, he probably would go to jail.
But at that time, we took it that he loved me, and he wanted to prevent me from drowning.
So that was my childhood.
Well, I had some Jewish education.
As poor as we were, my-- my father hired a Jewish teacher.
He was a very orthodox man.
And he used to come, like, twice a week to the house to teach me to write Jewish and to pray.
We did not pay him any money, because he was probably poorer than we were, but when he came, my mother used to give him a plate of soup and a piece of bread.
And he--
No, I don't remember his name.
But one-- one day, he came.
And I was reading the prayers-- I probably didn't pronounce it good, or I didn't want to listen-- and he slapped me.
And that was it.
That was the end of my education.
I said, I never want to see him again.
And next time when I saw him coming through the courtyard, I used to run on the attic and hide.
And that was the end of it.
But little by little, I acquired some education by myself.
I continued, because as I grew older, I saw that I made a mistake.
Because wherever I went in the world, I always could have used the Jewish language, the Jewish prayers, and so forth.
Yes, I'm not religious.
I'm very nationalistic.
But I am Jewish at heart, and I celebrate Jewish holidays.
When-- oh, when I was a child, my mother was very religious, and so was my grandfather, my grandmother.
My father was conservative, but Jewish holidays was very strictly observed.
And so when I turned 15, I was engaged.
I was engaged to my mother's brother's son.
At that time in Europe, you were permitted to marry a cousin if it wasn't the same blood.
If that would be, my-- my-- father's brother's son, I wouldn't be permitted.
Being that it was my mother's brother's son, they considered it a different genes.
So I was permitted.
I was engaged to be married.
But my husband--
His name was Sam Ajzenberg, which he carried my mother's name because it was my mother's brother.
He was-- I was 15, and he was almost 20, 19 and a half.
We were not married because he knew that he's going to have to go to the army.
So he was waiting.
And soon enough, they took him to the army in-- in the end of 1936.
He-- he was in the army for two years.
And when he returned, it was the beginning of 1939.
So we decided to get married, and the wedding was set for August the 5th of 1939.
As it happened, it was close to three weeks before the second war.
My mother's mother lived in the [NON-ENGLISH.] Being that-- [NON-ENGLISH], N-A-S-I-E-L-S.
Her name was Jenta Ajzenberg.
Being that it was three weeks before the holidays, after we were married, my parents-- we had rented an apartment, but never lived in it.
Because It was three weeks before the holidays, my parents, especially my mother, insisted that we stay with them until after the holiday.
And then, we should go and live on our own.
We had only one room and a little kitchen.
But there was always room for everybody.
So we were preparing for the holidays.
And then, the first of September, the war broke out.
As the war broke out, in six days, Hitler occupied most Poland, except for Warsaw.
Hitler was-- we were surrounded for approximately [INAUDIBLE], between 2 and 1/2 and three weeks, I don't remember exactly.
Well, when the war broke out, we were in-- in our apartments.
All of a sudden, we heard a lot of aeroplanes.
So everybody ran down to the court house-- to the courtyard.
As we came to the courtyard, we saw all the aeroplanes.
Everybody was screaming, 'that is exercise,' which in Polish, is maneuvers.
And so everybody was happy that we having maneuvers.
But all of a sudden, about a few moments later, we looked across the river.
And everything was ablaze.
Flames were reaching up to the sky.
Everything was burning.

Then people began to scream, 'that is war.' And in the same moment, we saw all the German aeroplanes, the Masserschmitt, with the swastikas on their bellies going, you know, flying over our courtyard.
Well, at that time, I didn't really feel any fear.
First of all, I was only not-- not quite 19 years old.
Then my father used to be in the Russian army during the First World War.
And then, Germans also at that time occupied Poland.
And he was with the Germans.
He used to tell me that don't be afraid, because I live through them.
They-- the only thing they didn't commit any atrocities then.
So we had no fear.
But sure, we were wrong, because like I said, they had all Poland in six days.
They surrounded [NON-ENGLISH].
They cut off the electricity.
They cut off the water.
We had no food.
Everything was in ruins.
They were bombing us day and night.
And it was horrible.
When they started bombing, everybody thought that it would be safer in the Jewish center, because we lived by the river.
And there were bridges, and there was warehouses with ammunitions, armies.
They said they're going to be worse, because they're going to try to get the army's-- the army, the ammunition, the bridges.
So we all split.
My father had a sister in central Warsaw.
So my father took his mother and my two sisters, and he went to live with his sister.
I went with my husband to his sister who lived in the other part of town.
We were there, and they were bombing day and night.
One day, I remember there was screaming.
Somebody said that they are going to throw gas.
What we should do, go down to the basement.
And they say, take a rag and put some urine, and take it through your nose, because we are going to be gassed.
People didn't realize that that was really stupid, so that's what we did.
After that, we were in the basement.
We put urine on a rag and put it towards our noses, not to breathe the gas that supposedly Hitler was putting, you know, throwing.
But he never did.
But you know, people were afraid of every move.
I was in the Jewish center, which was [NON-ENGLISH], was close to [NON-ENGLISH] which is part [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were bombing day and night.
It was terrible.
I saw buildings swaying in front of my eyes and fell.
We were sure-- we were sure that we were going to be killed.
We had no connection with my family.
We didn't know what happened.
Well, Polish-- Poland was always antisemitic.
But being that I grew up in a [?
Gentile ?] neighborhood, I really didn't feel it.
I used to read sometimes in the newspaper, like shortly before the war, the students in the university-- they rebelled against the Jews.
They were saying don't buy in Jewish stores.
But I, personally, didn't feel it, because I lived a [?
Gentile ?] neighborhood.
Now, going back to that day, that we got up one day in the morning.
We didn't get undressed, or wash, or anything.
We just lived.
In the daytime, we were staying in the courtyards ready to run.
The night time, we would go down to the cellars.
And one-- after a few days, I said to my husband, I want to go to see if my family's still alive.
My husband got some place a little bit of sugar and some bread.
Because when they were bombing, the stores were open.
People were standing in front, the owners, in front of their stores.
And they were begging to take things so they shouldn't burn.
Take [INAUDIBLE].
Take jewelry.
Take food.
But nobody thought about it, because everybody was running for-- for their lives.
So who was thinking of taking jewelry or [INAUDIBLE] if you didn't know that the next moment you'll be alive?
So we got some sugar and bread in the pocket and some other things I don't remember.
And we were going from [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH], which was-- it was like a mini mall.
And it was a house.
It was actually a courtyard that connected two streets, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's where my aunt lived.
We would go-- we start going when it was still curfew.
We couldn't go, but we risked.
We left maybe about 4 o'clock, early in the morning.
I don't remember.
It was-- it was like a few days after the war broke out.
Maybe a week, because Warsaw was surrounded by about two and a half, between two and a half and three weeks.
The curfew was for everyone.
Not that I remember.
At that time, was curfew.
You couldn't-- couldn't walk.
But we didn't think about it, because I wanted to see if my parents and family were still alive.
So we were walking.
As we were walking early in the morning, I saw the German aeroplanes.
People were running from place to place.
They stooped down very low, and with machine guns, they used to, you know, spray the streets.
And there were people falling in front of us.
And I used to step over dead bodies until we got to my-- my aunt's house.
That was the first time I ever saw dead bodies.
I can't describe it, because I had no feeling.
We-- we were only in the mind to run to see what happened with my family.
So when we got there, I remember in that-- in the house-- in that house where my aunt lived, there was also a bakery.
So when we got there, we got everybody together.
And say, brought them some sugar and whatever we had in our pockets.
And I saw that the whole family's all right.
We were there maybe not even 10 minutes.
All of a sudden, the aeroplanes came down so low.
And they start bombing the whole yard and the bakery.
And I remember it was-- became dark.
And just like electricity would tear us apart.
My husband, being that he had some military training, he ran to the door, stretched his arm, and didn't let nobody leave.
He says, you're going to get killed.
Actually, we would, because the-- the windows, stones, everything was flying, you know.
It was terrible.
And all of it was in flames.
It was burning all around.
Of course, I was scared.
Well, it's hard for me, because it's been 50 some years.
So I sort of settled with it.
Of course, I was scared.
So we all-- we ran to one-- one side of the yard where it was-- you know, it would go through.
And it was already flames.
You couldn't go.
So people went to the other street instead to [NON-ENGLISH], we went to the other street to [NON-ENGLISH].
When we came there, in there was flames.
Everything all around was burning.
So my husband said, the only chance we might have if we run through the fire.
Otherwise, we are going to burn to death.
So my husband and myself, we took my grandma under her arms, and all the family as-- you know, the way everybody could.
I remember my mother was holding my both sisters by hand.
And we-- he says, we now count to three.
We ran.
And we ran through the fire.
I had my hair slightly singed, but we got through.
When we came out through the street, there was dead bodies all over.
There was craters from the bombs.
People were running.
I remember I saw a lady holding her little-- little child in her arms.
And screaming, thank god I saved my child.
And the child was dead.
And she was-- and she didn't see it, because she was in shock.
So we-- I remember we ran to another house which was already bombed out.
My husband said that that will be safer, because they're not going to bomb again.
And your father was also with you?
My father, everybody, ran.
Once we got to that house, my-- I remember my mother and my younger sister, with Regina, she stood someplace on the steps.
My father somehow-- my father somehow got in-- in the yard, in the middle of the yard.
I was standing by the entrance with my husband and my middle sister next to me.
All of a sudden, a bomb fell in the courtyard.
OK.
